exercises.title: int vs float
exercises.description: Understand the difference between integers and floats. Print the type of each value, then demonstrate that dividing two ints can produce a float.
exercises.starterCode:
a = 7
b = 2

# TODO: Print type of a
print(type(a))

# TODO: Print type of b
print(type(b))

# TODO: Print a / b (regular division)
print(a / b)

# TODO: Print type of a / b
print(type(a / b))

# TODO: Print a // b (floor division)
print(a // b)

# TODO: Print type of a // b
print(type(a // b))
hints: Regular division (/) always returns a float in Python 3.
Floor division (//) returns an int when both operands are ints.

exercises.title: Float Precision and Rounding
exercises.description: Floats can have tiny precision issues. Use round() and f-string formatting to control how numbers display.
exercises.starterCode:
pi = 3.14159265358979
price = 19.999999999

# TODO: Print pi rounded to 2 decimal places using round()
print(round(pi, 2))

# TODO: Print pi formatted to 4 decimal places using f-string :.4f
print(f"{pi:.4f}")

# TODO: Print price rounded to 2 decimal places
print(round(price, 2))

# TODO: Demonstrate float imprecision: print 0.1 + 0.2
print(0.1 + 0.2)

# TODO: Print round(0.1 + 0.2, 2)
print(round(0.1 + 0.2, 2))
hints: round(number, digits) rounds to the given number of decimal places.
f"{pi:.4f}" formats to exactly 4 decimal places.

exercises.title: Large Numbers and Underscores
exercises.description: Python supports underscores in numeric literals for readability. Use them to write large numbers clearly, then perform calculations.
exercises.starterCode:
# Python allows underscores in numbers for readability
world_population = 8_000_000_000   # 8 billion
speed_of_light = 299_792_458       # meters per second
data_budget_mb = 15_000
file_size_mb = 250

# TODO: Print world_population
print(world_population)

# TODO: Print speed_of_light
print(speed_of_light)

# TODO: Calculate and print how many files fit in data_budget
files_that_fit = data_budget_mb // file_size_mb
print(files_that_fit)

# TODO: Print 2 ** 32 (max value of a 32-bit unsigned int)
print(2 ** 32)
hints: Underscores are ignored by Python — 8_000_000_000 is the same as 8000000000.
// gives a whole number result (floor division).

warmup.title: Quick Recall: int and float
warmup.description: Which operations produce int vs float? Predict then run.
warmup.starterCode:
print(type(10))       # int
print(type(10.0))     # float
print(type(10 / 2))   # always float in Python 3
print(type(10 // 2))  # int when both sides are int
